trend_name: Sally Beauty Drops ion 24K: A 3-Minute Damage Fix Built for Bleach Battles
description:
Damage control just got a new contender.Sally Beautyis expanding its innovation pipeline with ion’s newest collection, ion 24K, a four-piece lineup aimed squarely at one of the biggest client complaints in the chair: fried hair.
The hook is a three-minute damage-reversal claim powered by the brand’s TrichoPower molecular technology, designed to help bring hair back to a more natural state from root to tip. According to Sally Beauty, the Rapid Recovery system can help maintain up to 87% of hair’s natural foundation after bleaching, a stat that will get any colorist’s attention.
The collection includes four formulas built to tackle damage, buildup and color prep:
The launch comes as hair damage climbs the consumer priority list; 40% of U.S. beauty consumers say repairing damage is a top concern, according to a 2025 study cited by the retailer.
The positioning is classic Sally. The company is leaning into pro-inspired formulas without the pro-only price tag. The brand says shoppers can save more than 60% versus a leading competitor on the Rapid Recovery treatment.
Ion 24K is available now on SallyBeauty.com and in the Sally Beauty app, with in-store availability beginning February 9, 2026.
The company also teased another launch. Sally Beauty says a new ion styling tool collection promising smoother, shinier hair without harsh heat is expected this spring, with details to be announced later.

trend_name: K18 Drops a Purple Shampoo That Goes After Brass at the Source
description:
Blonde pros, take note:K18is coming for brass—and not just with more purple pigment.
The brand has unveiled TripleBright Oxidation Defense Purple Shampoo, a new toning cleanser engineered to tackle the actual chemistry behind dull, oxidized blonde hair. Instead of simply masking yellow tones, the formula works in three steps: remove, neutralize and defend.
First up: ionic detox. The shampoo is designed to strip away up to three months of mineral and hard-water buildup in a single wash, helping restore light reflection and clarity to blonde hair that’s gone a little muddy.
Next, precision violet technology steps in to neutralize yellow tones—without the heavy pigment dump that can leave hair looking gray, flat or accidentally lavender. The violet pigments are delivered in a lightweight foam, giving stylists more controlled, even distribution from root to tip.
Finally, a protective biopolymer helps shield hair from future mineral and metal buildup, working to keep blonde brighter between salon visits.
According to Suveen Sahib, brassiness isn’t just a color problem—it’s a chemistry problem. Oxidation and mineral buildup distort tone over time, especially after lightening services. TripleBright aims to reset that process at the source.
The shampoo is designed to complement the brand’s repair lineup, including K18REPAIR and the K18 Molecular Repair Hair Mask, pairing surface-level brightness with internal bond repair.
Bonus for busy colorists: the foam format won’t stain hands, towels or the shampoo bowl—and it rinses clean without over-toning drama.
TripleBright Oxidation Defense Purple Shampoo hits Sephora online March 23, with in-store andK18Hair.comavailability beginning March 27. For blonding specialists tired of fighting brass with blunt purple pigment, this launch promises a more science-forward fix.

Danger Jonesis helping professional colorists take control of their toning with its newest launch: the Artistic Toners. These cream-based progressive toners can be used for any bleach and tone service or gray-blending techniques.
Whether used alone or mixed for custom color results, combine any toner with Danger Jones 5 Volume Deluxe Cream Developer, process for 5-20 minutes and achieve stunning shades from natural blonde to icy platinum or soft silver.
These alkaline-based progressive toners with bonder will be available in three core shades:Danger Jones is helping professional colorists take control of their toning with its newest launch: the Artistic Toners. These cream-based progressive toners can be used for any bleach and tone service or gray-blending techniques.Courtesy of Danger Jones
For more on how to use the toners, check out this video from Danger Jones Director of Education Doug Theo.

trend_name: Danger Jones Lowers Prices of Lighteners
description:
Danger Jones is lowering the price of its lightening products in solidarity with hair professionals struggling in the current economy. The price change is expected to last through 2025.
The brand's premium powder lightener is dropping in price from around $30 to $23 USD and the corresponding developers are being reduced by $2 USD.

trend_name: Here's How Lisap Milano Is Helping Stylists Connect and Create Healthy Hair
description:
In this exclusiveBeauty Launchpadpodcast, sponsored byLisap Milano, we’re sitting down with Sonali Pai, founder of Grapefruit Beauty Collective (Lisap Milano's parent company). As a woman and minority business owner, Pai offers insight into the challenges she’s overcome, the victories she’s achieved and the vital role representation plays in leadership within the beauty industry. She also provides an exciting glimpse into Lisap Milano's upcoming product launches and discusses how Grapefruit is fostering and building community at trade shows and events. Together, we explore the latest hair and beauty trends and their impact on the future of the industry. Plus, so much more!
Don’t miss this inspiring conversation—and be sure to stick around until the end for an exclusive 30% discount code on select products atshoplisap.com!

We’ve always heard it in school, but it’s absolutely true when they say your clients are showing up for their appointments beyond getting their hair done. Theconnection you sharewith the people you have in your chair is unlike anything I’ve ever experienced. They are truly part of you. We laugh together as much as we cry together. We share stories of our lives and secrets we swore never to tell a soul. The trust in that kind of connection goes beyond business services, but is rooted in the need for community. We need friends and people we can trust to show up when we are in need. Being a bridge to someone’s self-confidence starts with the connection you have with them—not just the hair you can give them. I can truly say that every connection I’ve shared with my long-time clients has been some of my closest friendships, and for that I’m truly grateful."I can truly say that every connection I’ve shared with my long-time clients has been some of my closest friendships, and for that I’m truly grateful."Courtesy of Lynda Peterson

One thing I wish the world could experience just once would be a hair show. Specifically,The Rainbow Convention. If they could see how the artists of the hair industry can show up for each other, their hearts would grow at least three sizes. There is nothing more beautiful than watching a bunch of humans who LOVE WHAT THEY DO come together and support one another. For a long time, people assumed that hairstylists weren’t as supportive of one another as they should be, but I don’t agree. Don’t get me wrong, my journey hasn’t been the smoothest, but I’m so grateful for every connection I ever shared with any beauty professional. We understand each other in ways no one else ever could, and when we don’t, we aim to seek the answer. We try to move with integrity and love, and that has been one of the most healing aspects of my career that I know I will never find anywhere else. The hair community can be truly beautiful.

"There is nothing more beautiful than watching a bunch of humans who LOVE WHAT THEY DO come together and support one another."Courtesy of Lynda PetersonOKAY. Um… HOW COOL??? I seriously fight demons, trying not to talk to every person who’ll listen ears off about the technology and science behind what we do, especially when it comes to chemical services. This industry has come so far over the years, and I’m blown away by some cool new gadget or product every day. I’m very honored and privileged to be a part of theOLAPLEX Pro Collective, where I also learn so much about the science behind the scenes. Every time they share something with us, I swear I hear symphonies.

Becoming a hairstylist changed my life completely. When I started hair school, I knew I was finally doing something for myself, but I didn’t realize the gravity of my decision. I was a teen mom, having my first child at 15, and also raised in a single-parent household. My mother had immigrated from Panama and provided for my brothers and me completely on her own. When she wasn’t home, it was my older brother's and my responsibility to care for my nonverbal, highly dependent, autistic and epileptic little brother. My whole life, all I knew was taking care of those around me and never myself. When I signed up to attend hair school, I sobbed. I had no idea what I was getting into, but I knew it was the first thing I ever did for myself that was worth something. I learned so many things about myself and my perseverance in my career, overcoming obstacles placed in my path. Being in this industry, you have so many opportunities to look inward to challenge yourself to grow. I went from doing hair in an old trailer to becoming a successfully self-employed hairstylist, indie educator and hairfluencer. So much can change for you, so long as you are willing to work for it.

trend_name: Daniel Mora Discusses His New The Hair Shop Collaboration Smart Weft HIEWS
description:
Beauty Launchpadrecently attendedInternational Beauty Show New York(IBS NY), which featured an expanded lineup of education, speakers and exhibitors who shared valuable industry knowledge and their latest innovative launches. One sector of the hair and beauty industry that had a heavy presence at the show was hair extensions, and it's no surprise considering how much that market is growing!
Hair extensionsoffer an accessible solution for everyone—from celebrities and clients seeking full glam looks to those dealing with hair loss. They provide an empowering way to boost confidence and help wearers feel their absolute best. With the growth of this market comes an exciting wave of innovation.The Hair Shopis among the leading brands recognizing the need for hair extensions to evolve, catering to the ever-changing demands of both hair professionals and their clients. With its new launchSmart Weft HIEWS x Daniel Mora, the brand is helping hair professionals be more efficient behind the chair by eliminating the need to color extensions.
While at IBS NY, Managing Editor Gabby Bach chatted with Daniel Mora (@danielmbeauty) at The Hair Shop (@thehairshopinc) booth to learn more about the HIEWS launch, what sets it apart from other extension options and so much more! Check out the full interview below.

Color psychology has long suggested that hues influence perception, energy levels, and internal emotional states. According to research in behavioral aesthetics (Saito, 1996; Elliot & Maier, 2012), warm colors can increase stimulation, while cooler shades tend to calm the nervous system. When hair becomes the canvas, the result is not just psychological — it’s deeply personal.
Clients rarely choose a new color randomly. A woman opting for platinum blonde after divorce or copper red during career change isn’t just chasing a trend, she’s crafting a new narrative. Hair color becomes an extension of emotional energy: blondes evoke softness and reinvention, brunettes often signify grounded strength and vivid shades (red, purple, blue) can communicate rebellion, healing or creative awakening. In my practice, clients often report improved confidence and mood post-color service. Some describe the effect as “liberating” or “clarifying,” especially after long emotional stagnation.

Beyond emotional shifts, the coloring process affects the client physiologically. Sitting still, being touched gently, receiving attention and observing gradual transformation in the mirror — all these factors engage the parasympathetic nervous system. It's a known mechanism in neuroaesthetics and somatic therapy: visual and tactile beauty rituals can lower cortisol, reduce muscle tension and increase dopamine (Kawabata & Zeki, 2004).
Moreover, healthy hair after coloring can directly support self-image. On the contrary, chemically damaged hair may lead to frustration, anxiety or even withdrawal from social interaction. That’s why professional products, scalp care and controlled technique are essential.
As stylists, we work not just with color, but with keratin structure, scalp pH, oxidative balance and most importantly: with the psychological state of our clients.

trend_name: 3. The Stylist as a Mirror Guide
description:
Modern hairdressers are not just technical executors. We are aesthetic advisors, emotional listeners and sometimes silent therapists. Many clients open up during the sessions about grief, self-doubt, relationships and trauma. They leave not only with new hair but with visible lightness.
A stylist who listens deeply and suggests color based on emotional tone, not just skin undertone, can help catalyze profound internal change. For example: offering golden tones to a client grieving or soft lowlights for a woman rebuilding confidence — this is intuitive psychology in practice. This is especially important when dealing with clients recovering from illness, postpartum depression, loss or major life shifts.

The coloring process must be safe: physically and emotionally. Clients trust us with their appearance, but often, unconsciously, also with their emotional fragility. Any mistake, such as over-processing or color mismatch, can have emotional consequences beyond mere inconvenience.
That’s why education, ethics and empathy are fundamental. Hair rituals also have a symbolic layer: cutting off the past, softening pain or marking a new cycle. In many cultures, hair is associated with life force or transformation (anthropologically and spiritually). Stylists, knowingly or not, act as ritual facilitators.

Hair coloring is a multidimensional experience. It merges science and emotion, chemistry and art, identity and healing. When practiced with awareness and integrity, it becomes far more than fashion — it becomes therapy through transformation.
Stylists today must understand the silent depth behind each color request. To color hair is, in many cases, to help color a new chapter in someone’s life.

The key to all successful hair color is an in-depth consultation, where you ask and answer questions. It is important that the guest/client has a clear understanding of process, commitment, cost and maintenance of their desired result."The key to all successful hair color is an in-depth consultation, where you ask and answer questions. It is important that the guest/client has a clear understanding of process, commitment, cost and maintenance of their desired result."Courtesy of Cherry Petenbrink
Get to know the client's hair history to ensure your success. Previous color applications can cause major issues when decolorizing.
Have a plan to create perfect pastels and follow up appointments that map out the maintenance and follow up appointments.

Removing pigment, whether natural or artificial, is a crucial part of a successful Perfect Platinum. Choosing the best option of bleach/lightener and developer is key to the damage free lift you need. I recommend using a bond multiplier that can be added to your mixture or applied to the hair prior to starting this step.
Trust the process. Under processing can cause uneven tones, with too much yellow or yellow/orange left in the hair. Banding (uneven lift) is hard to balance. Your goal is to achieve the palest of yellow level 10. I love a charcoal lightener to achieve this — it tones as it lightens.
Replace and replenish as you work. I choose to strengthen and replace essential amino acids as I work. Doing a deep penetrating treatment mask between bleaching and toning to equal pH and porosity.
Choosing a “toner” can assist in the anchor of pastel pigment. If your goal is soft pink, lavender or coral, demi-permanent color can be used as a filler.

Neutralize the lift you have achieved. You want a clean and clear canvas to showcase a perfect pastel. Using purple shampoo and conditioner is the first step. This may be enough, depending on the remaining residual pigment in the hair. You can choose a demi-permanent toner, gloss or glaze that neutralizes/deposits a translucent shade that aligns with your final pastel shade choice.

"Like any other color application, there are many techniques that can be used for desired results. Horizontal placement versus vertical placement will create very different results."Courtesy of Cherry PetenbrinkLike any other color application, there are many techniques that can be used for desired results. Horizontal placement versus vertical placement will create very different results.

Work with small thin sections. Saturation is key. Use your index and middle finger, like scissors up and down strands, to work the color into the hair.
If you are using multiple colors, be sure to blur the lines between color shift (creates another color). This will keep your application seamless and beautiful once styled.
Process according to manufacturer’s recommended time. Rinse until clear with cool water.Maintenance is crucial for pastel-colored hair to extend the longevity of the shade.Courtesy of Cherry Petenbrink

Wait at least three days to shampoo. Shampoo less frequently. Use an SLS- and SLES-free shampoo. Use a pastel color conditioner to refresh and replenish pigment between salon visits. I love to make custom color depositing masks. Use cool water when rinsing.
Protect hair from the sun by wearing a hat. Turn the heat down on your styling tools. Use a heat protectant before blow outs and hot tool styling.
Use serum or oil for additional shine. Use a bond building leave-in treatment to keep hair strong and hydrated.
About the Author:
Cherry Petenbrink (@its.me.cherryp) is an independent hair artist and consultant based in Houston, TX. She is also the Artistic Director for Olivia Garden. In 2012, Cherry used her hair color expertise in the blockbuster filmThe Hunger Games. Her hair color talent attributes to other big screen characters, including Scarlet Johannsson, Jennifer Lawrence, Emma Thompson, Annette Benning, Nicole Kidman, Michelle Pfeiffer, Julia Roberts, Tom Hanks, Colin Farrell and Mindy Kaling, among many other highly acclaimed actors who have trusted her with multiple personal and on-screen character looks.
